commands.review_summary:
The PR changes the attack-surface summary so the browser-control line accounts for plugin allow/deny, disabled browser entries, global plugin disablement, and explicit browser config, with regression tests for those policy cases.

PR surface: Source +30, Tests +46. Total +76 across 2 files.

Reproducibility: yes. Source inspection shows current main defaults the summary line to browser control enabled when browser config is omitted, without checking restrictive plugin policy.
commands.review_followup: - No automated repair is needed; the remaining action is ordinary maintainer review of a clean, narrow PR.

target.title: fix(security): report browser audit summary from plugin policy
target.body:
Related: #86138, #76631, #96397, #92086, #92883

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where `openclaw security audit` could report `browser control: enabled` in the attack-surface summary when a restrictive plugin policy excluded or denied the bundled browser plugin and no browser config was present.

That output is confusing for hardened deployments because the summary line contradicts the effective plugin policy operators use to reduce agent/browser attack surface.

## Why This Change Was Made

The attack-surface summary now derives the browser-control line from the effective browser/plugin policy instead of treating missing `browser` config as enabled. It keeps current startup-intent behavior: explicit `browser` config still reports enabled, while `plugins.deny`, `plugins.entries.browser.enabled=false`, `browser.enabled=false`, and `plugins.enabled=false` report disabled.

This is intentionally a narrow audit-output fix. It does not change plugin loading, browser startup, tool exposure, Security Matrix semantics, or agent runtime behavior.

## User Impact

Operators using `plugins.allow`/`plugins.deny` as a hardening control get a more accurate attack-surface summary. A read-only or minimal deployment that excludes browser no longer sees the summary claim browser control is enabled solely because of config defaults.

## Evidence

Focused tests:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/security/audit-summary.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       6 passed (6)

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/security/audit-extra.sync.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       14 passed (14)
```

Lint/format checks:

```text
pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/security/audit-extra.summary.ts src/security/audit-summary.test.ts
All matched files use the correct format.

node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/security/audit-extra.summary.ts src/security/audit-summary.test.ts
git diff --check -- src/security/audit-extra.summary.ts src/security/audit-summary.test.ts
```

Patched collector proof for the hardened shape:

```text
summary.attack_surface
groups: open=0, allowlist=0
tools.elevated: enabled
hooks.webhooks: disabled
hooks.internal: disabled
browser control: disabled
trust model: personal assistant (one trusted operator boundary), not hostile multi-tenant on one shared gateway
```

Related-work boundary:

- #86138 owns agent-facing `plugins.bundledDiscovery` hardening.
- #96397 owns startup warnings for configured channel plugins that cannot load.
- #92086 owns the broader observe-only Security Matrix runtime-fact model.
- #92883 is the closest merged precedent: a narrow security-audit predicate fix with focused regression coverage.

This PR only fixes the browser-control line in `summary.attack_surface`.
target.author: amtellezfernandez
